does: This paper systematically evaluates metric reliability in multimodal machine unlearning for Vision-Language Models, analyzing five standard metrics (Forget Accuracy, Retain Accuracy, Membership Inference Attack, Activation Distance, and JS divergence) across three VQA benchmarks. Through extensive experiments on LLaVA-1.5-7B and BLIP-2 OPT-2.7B models, the authors demonstrate that these metrics produce conflicting rankings of unlearning methods, with Kendall tau correlation analysis revealing two opposing clusters with near-zero agreement. The paper identifies that multimodal VQA exhibits significantly lower metric agreement (tau = 0.086) compared to unimodal classification (tau = 0.158), showing that joint image-text processing amplifies evaluation inconsistency. To address this fragmentation, the paper introduces the Unified Quality Score (UQS), a composite metric that weights individual metrics by their Spearman correlation with an oracle distance measure derived from comparing the unlearned model to a fully retrained baseline.
connects: This work responds to the growing challenge of evaluating machine unlearning methods, a problem that has intensified as unlearning becomes legally required under GDPR and other privacy regulations. It extends prior work on unlearning evaluation in unimodal settings by highlighting how multimodal architectures create new evaluation challenges. The paper builds on established unlearning benchmarks and metrics from the literature while drawing connections to broader research on metric reliability in machine learning evaluation. The study engages with prior systematic analyses of metric disagreement in related domains and leverages oracle-based validation approaches from evaluation methodology research. By focusing specifically on Vision-Language Models, it addresses a relatively understudied area where the interplay between vision and language pathways creates unique evaluation complications not present in single-modality unlearning.
enables: This work establishes a foundation for more reliable evaluation of multimodal machine unlearning by proposing the Unified Quality Score as a principled alternative to fragmented metric-based assessment. The systematic characterization of metric failure modes in multimodal settings enables future research to better understand which evaluation approaches are trustworthy for different types of unlearning scenarios. The identification of pathway-specific metric disagreement suggests future work on architecture-aware evaluation frameworks tailored to multimodal models. The paper creates opportunities for developing improved metrics that better capture unlearning quality in joint vision-language spaces, and opens questions about whether similar metric conflicts arise in other multimodal tasks beyond VQA. The UQS framework provides a methodological template for composite metric design that could extend to other domains where metric disagreement plagues evaluation, and the systematic analysis approach models how to rigorously assess evaluation reliability in emerging model architectures and safety-critical applications.
